sentences.text: Yes.
I did learn basically, Hebrew.
It helped me that I knew Jewish letters, alphabet.
And also it helped me that there was the only language I could communicate with the other people that came from all over the world.
And I became quite liked by the people from kibbutz.
They even tried to match me up quietly, that I work with somebody who is not married.
Because they wanted me to stay in the kibbutz.
But unfortunately, my Aunt Hanka, who came from Poland with her Christian husband, and her two children, she was in the Ma'abarot.
Ma'abarot, it was a temporary settling of Jews--
--in [INAUDIBLE], in Lodz, where the airport is.
And when she found out where I am, she came to the kibbutz, and tried to do her own thing, like telling me that what you're going to do here?
After that-- after the kibbutz, you're going to go to the army.
And you know what's in the army?
The army, there are so many men.
And they use the woman.
You cannot go to the army.
You have to get married.
And the best thing if you married a-- an American tourist.
Then you can bring out your father, then we can join you.
She was putting all the cards on me that I'm gonna save her from Israel.
Because obviously, my Aunt Roma was not anxious to bring her in.
And she was to take me to that Ma'abarot where she was, or to Tel Aviv.
And I think that I was in shock, that I let her do whatever she wanted.
Because leaving Poland, leaving my own environment, was very hard for me, not knowing the new language, not being in home, so many changes.
This was not the first time I'm being uprooted.
Very seldom I talked to them.
Very seldom I visited them.
Because she did not want me to come and visit.
So I saw them only a few times.
And most of the time this aunt was coming with all kinds of ideas, who she going to introduce me to, with all kind of tourists from United States.
That was her plan to better her life.
And one day, probably I must have been very depressed, because I went with her.
She matched me up with this American tourist from Poland.
And as I found out later, she got $500 for it.
And she was also planning that when we arrived there, and I'll be there, she'll come easily to United States.
Because she did not like Israel.
In Israel, when she was preaching, because she took a new religion on herself.
She was a Jehovah Witness.
The people who lived around her, they were really angry with her.
Because she was going and preaching from door to door.
And some people there were throwing tomatoes at her or eggs.
And they were really angry with her.
And she could not do it anymore.
He-- he was a Pole from Catholic family.
But he did not mix into anything.
He just say, leave me alone.
He was a decent person.
He did not tell her to go to church.
Do whatever you want.
Be whoever you want.
He was a quiet man who got a very good job right away in Israel, because he was a technician, a model maker.
And he was working for the Israeli industry of airplanes.
Well, I imagine that they had no religion.
Because everybody was making fun of her.
So did the children.
And when they came to Israel, they wanted to identify with Israeli children.
And they went to school.
And I believed that they did not pick up anything from my aunt.
Because still today, they are not Jehovah's Witnesses.
Actually both of them got married to Jewish girls.
Well, when my aunt introduced him to me, it was like she talked for me.
She told me, this is the man you going to marry.
Don't think that anybody will like you.
Look at yourself, how you look.
You have three hair on your head.
You have two sticks instead legs, two hands-- two sticks instead hands.
She said, what do you think, you're a beauty?
She put me down completely.
And then she said, if you go to the United States, at least you gonna have a good life.
Probably, if he was telling the truth, he must have been at least 18 years older than I was.
And I went like a sheep to the butcher.
I did get married.
And in a few days, he left, because he had some business to attend in United States.
And when he left, I kind of woke up out of the shock.
And I said, there is no way I'm gonna go to the United States.
And I was looking for a way, you know, to get out of it.
I didn't know the language.
I didn't know anybody who would cooperate with me.
They all said, yeah, yeah, yeah, go.
My father was-- he was thrilled that I'm gonna go to United States, everybody in the family.
If I would say no, that they were angry with me.
And really, when I said to my father, I'm not gonna go to United States, he got angry at me and never talk to me for the longest time.
So I went in Netanya.
I lived in Netanya with his family.
And in Netanya, I met a girlfriend who was also from Poland.
And I had a camera.
And we went to the photographer there with the camera.
And I started to talk to him.
And she introduced me to him.
He was a-- no, he was a divorced man with an older child.
The child was nine year old.
I was 18 at that time.
And he was very sympathetic to my story, and whatever.
And I got to meet him and talk to him.
And after awhile I wrote my husband that I'm not coming to United-- I want a divorce.
And he tried to convince me.
But after I said strongly, I don't want.
He came, and he gave me a divorce.
So I stayed with him only a few days.
When I got divorced, I had no place to go.
My aunt didn't want to talk to me.
My parents didn't want to talk to me.
I had no friends around.
Everybody was in really hard situation.
A new commerce too, Israel, you know how life-- the weather was very hot.
So I was going on the street of Tel Aviv to agencies to find a job.
And I found my first job.
Yes.
I went to Tel Aviv, because there was more opportunities there.
And I went there to organize-- like an employment offices.
I went there.
And I found a job with some men who was making in-- in light bulbs.
He was cutting them, little pictures, you know, little snow falling, you know, those things that they sell in the flea market, whatever.
And I stayed with him.
And then one day, I rode on a-- on a bus.
And I met a man who spoke Russian and Polish.
And said-- he paid attention to me.
Because when I asked for directions, he said, I'm going the same way.
And this man told me why-- I told him that I have no place to live.
He said, why don't you go to a place like YMCA?
There was places like that in Israel where single men and single women live.
And I took his advice.
And I lived there for awhile.
And I worked for his aunt.
He introduced me as his girlfriend.
But that wasn't true.
We both knew that.
But because I was his girlfriend, the lady gave me a job as a seamstress.
She would teach me seamstress.
But for a long while, she did not teach me seamstress.
I had to go and ask her clients to pay whatever they owe her.
And the money I was making was for breakfast.
That's the only money I was making.
I could only buy a glass of milk and a roll.
That's all I ate.
And sometimes I ate in her house, whatever, you know, I could.
And I remember also that-- that cousin, her-- her-- her-- the man that I met, he went to a school for-- to work on a boat.
Because he was a chef in Russia.
He told me he himself spent years in-- in prison in Russia.
That's why he was sympathetic in helping everybody.
And at that time, when I was in Tel Aviv, living, Mr.
Stein, who I met as a photographer, came to visit, looking for me.
And he found me there.
And since, you know, that's what I wanted.
He was the only friend I had.
I married him, in spite that he had a grownup son already nine year old.
And I did not know even how to raise a child.
But I agreed that I will.
His child was with his sister-in-law.
Since the sister-in-law felt like his mother, she did not want to give him away.
So I lived with Mr.
Stein.
And then I had right away my own child.
Because I wanted somebody of my own.
And I had this beautiful child, Ester, who I gave the name after my natural mother.
And I lived a life of Israel.
But my husband was a photographer.
And in that studio that we had, also his brother worked.
And it was not enough to feed both families.
So my husband, Stein, went to-- to the American embassy and signed up to immigrate.
It was really almost like a joke.
Because his cousin did it, he did it.
He was sending application.
And a year after, we getting a call from the embassy in Tel Aviv.
And we go there.
And they want us.
And we decided we have to go.
But who is going to give me a visa?
In between my aunt from Chicago, Roma, sponsored Hanka, not knowing that Hanka is a Jehovah Witness.
They got into a fight.
And Roma didn't talk to Hanka.
When I wrote to my Aunt Roma, that I want to come, she said, why don't you write to Hanka?
She's the one who made your life miserable.
Let her pay for it.
And Hanka, out of guilt, sent me an affidavit.
She couldn't make the affidavit here.
So she went to one of her sisters and brothers of Jehovah Witnesses, and they are really nice people.
They did it for her.
They signed, you know, an affidavit that I can come.
And they will be responsible for me.
And I came with my husband and my daughter.
And we came to Chicago, where we stayed for a while.
And unfortunately, my husband did not learn very well English.
And he was-- he was only-- he only knew one profession, photographer.
And I, at that time, started to think of learning a little more than just being a housewife.
And I started my night courses.
I was going, and first of all, I learned how to drive.
And I kind of grew apart from him.
Because we are also a generation apart.
My husband was 14 years older than me.
And I thought to myself, that when my daughter will be bat mitzvah, I'll divorce him.
Because I wasn't happy.
He never did.
The son, when he finished high school, he went to the Israeli army and remained in Israel, where he got a very good education, a very good position.
I understand he's a diplomat.
And he's very well off in Israel.
While I was going here to school, I studied bookkeeping.
I took all kinds of courses to learn what was important and what was going well, for a person who doesn't know very well English.
So I learned bookkeeping and data processing.
And I had quite good success with it.
And every time I changed jobs, I got a better job, a better position.
And my daughter grew, and so grew me and my husband apart.
And we finally got divorced in 1971 when Ester was bat mitzvah.
I took a trip to Israel with her, so she'll get to know everything about Israel, about her roots.
And I was divorced for four years.
Then I met Sam Kersz, which I met in Oakton College.
He was having trouble with his family.
And we talked about it.
Actually, I was not very impressed in the beginning.
He was-- he looked kind of skinny.
And how to say?
He was not my type at that time.
That's what I figured out.
But when I got to know him, we felt like two [? neshumas ?] together, sister and brother.
He was also a Holocaust survivor.
I did not realize.
That's what brought us even closer because we had something in common.
His biography was almost parallel to my biography.
And when we talked about some subject, we understood it very well.
Sam was born in France, in Paris.
He lost both parents.
But he survived like I, with Christian people, with his sister.
And he came to Israel with exodus, with the boat.
And he was almost Israeli.
He was also raised in a kibbutz.
Yes.
He did.
He survived in France.
And when we met, of course, we found a lot in common.
And we got married 1976.
And Ester accepted him almost like a father.
They are very good friends.
And we are, knock on wood, happily married already for 19 years.
In May we'll be 20 years.
We're gonna to celebrate it.
But still, in spite of all that, I felt that something is missing in my life, especially when my daughter, after she finished schooling.
She got a bachelor degree.
And then she got a master degree in business administration, which I'm very proud.
Because no one of my family gotten that far.
But I insisted.
I said, Ester, you must study.
I don't care if you know how to cook, how to clean.
I don't care.
You have to study.
And that was her whole effort and my effort.
What she studied was my dream probably.
So she did finish school with honors and got right away a very good position.
Ester is very bright and smart.
I take advice from my daughter now.
She has two children.
I have two grandchildren, which is a miracle of itself that our family is getting larger.
But when Ester married and she moved out, not far, thank goodness, I felt really alone.
I was thinking about my sister and my brother.
Wouldn't it be nice, you know, for holidays, I could invite them, could have other people here.
When I heard people have cousins club, I said, God, what a wonderful idea!
But where am I going to get all the cousins from?

And I felt, always like there was something missing in my life, like a hand or leg, or-- And I was thinking about this brother of mine, that my father told me that I had a twin brother, and older sister, Genia.
And in Poland, one of my aunt's daughter, Basia, the one who is in Brazil, she hid one of the pictures of our family that she had in the attic.
And after the war she came there, and she found it.
So I had a picture of my sister, and my father, and my mother.
But I still didn't have the picture of Adam, my twin brother.
When I came to United States, we had some family that lived here long before the war.
And obviously, they got a picture of my mother with the children sitting on her lap, both twins, which I recognized.
I figured out which one is the girl, which one is the boy.
And every time I look at the picture, I was thinking, where is my twin?
What happened?
If I survived, there has to be possibility that one of them at least survived.
And then I thought, they had to survive together.

Because the things I heard about my sister, that she was a very smart-- you know, street smart girl, they told me, the landlady, when I came and visit her, that Genia could go in front of the long line, and sneak in, and get her portion of food, or milk, or whatever, and bring to the house like that.
This is a really hard question.
Because Adam asked me the same thing.
And I don't remember father looking for anybody else.
I think he believed that they did not survive.
In 1949, he made me a birth certificate, but never made a birth certificate for Adam.
He was like a non-existing, and-- or-- or for my sister.
He thought that if my sister was alive, she would know where she lived.
She would come back.
And my brother is probably not alive, because he was a boy.
That was his theory.
When I went to school to get my-- you know--
--diploma every year, I had to have my birth certificate.
So finally, he went to-- he wrote somebody to Sosnowiec, a witness from our neighborhood, some neighbor, and asked to go to court and be witness that I was born in 1939, May 3rd, and so on.
But there was very little mention about my brother, only that I am from twins.
So that's how I-- after the war, got my birth certificate.
He said before that, in the papers from the court, it said that he had no time to make the-- the birth certificates for the children, because he was going to the army.
That's how ended with that.
And unfortunately, later, I'll tell what happened to Adam's birth certificate, which was none.
So when I was alone here, I started to get more interested in my past.
And every city that I went on vacation, or wherever, I looked through all the telephone books looking for the name of Paluch, to see if maybe, maybe somebody survived.
It got to that point that I wanted every name in United States, Paluch, to check out.
And I had a friend, a detective, who gave me a listing.
And I wrote to all those people.
Some responded.
Some didn't.
And most of them were not Jewish.
The name Paluch did not belong, really, to a Jewish family.
And as time was passing by, I joined a Holocaust children survivor group.
And then I went to the Hidden Children gathering in 1991, in New York, where I put on a billboard, in the hotel, the picture that I had of the twins, us, with my sister.
And everybody else did put pictures.
And I was asking, anybody knows, please contact me.
But instead to find somebody that knows me, I found somebody who knew my husband from orphanage.
And I told him about it.
And he discovered his friends.
And I still did not know about whereabouts my brother.
But every of my friends and-- and to relatives, whatever, knew that I am still believing that Adam is somewhere there, or with Genia.
Even when I walk on the street, I look at people faces.
And I think, are they Jewish?
Are they maybe from Poland?
Is this my brother?
He would be that age as I am.
Every birthday, I remembered that I'm the only one celebrating my birthday.

And a friend of mine from Poland yet, my classmate, she sent me this article where there was a picture of a man standing in a Jewish synagogue in Warsaw, and asking in that article if anybody knows him.
The name was Yesha Doepski.
As it happened, a year ago, when my aunt died, Hanka, I went to San Diego.
Because I knew she was of possession of the picture of my grandparents on my mother's side that she never wanted to part with.
She wouldn't even take it to a photographer to make a copy.
She did not trust anyone.
So I went there.
And my uncle, who survived, her husband, he make me a beautiful copy on brown paper.
And I have this picture.
And I put it on the wall here in the room.
And every time I pass by, I looked at him.
And I got quite familiarized with his face.
And when I saw this article, and I saw Yesha Doepski face, I realized, this is my brother.
He was identical face with my grandfather.
Something in me told me this is it.
Because I saw many survivors.
I saw many faces.
It never struck me that it could be even.
When I saw this picture, I knew.
I sat on this information for four months.
I was wasting four months.
But I still didn't believe myself that it can-- such a miracle happen to me.
I knew it was him.
And now I have to do something about it.
So after New Year's, even a little before, I wrote a few letters to find out who was the reporter who went to Poland and interviewed the Jewish Holocaust survivors.
And she called me on January 13.
And she gave me his address.
And I asked her, what kind of man is he?
She said, very nice.
Then I asked her, how could I get his phone number?
She told me, there's a joint organization in Krakow.
Call this man.
He knows almost all the Jewish people in Poland.
So I called him immediately at work.
And he gave me my brother's phone number.
And I called.
When I called, the children answered.
And I told them, this is maybe a strange phone call, but I think that your father is my brother.
And there was quiet there.
The children were flabbergasted.
They called, mother, mother, come to the phone.
And his wife came to the phone.
And I said, this is maybe unbelievable to you, but I believe that your husband is my brother.
And she was not too much surprised.
She said, you know what?
He's very interested in your phone call.
Please call again.
He's not here.
And she said, he might be very late.
I said, no matter when he comes, I'll give you my phone number.
Tell him to call me.
And when I came home from work, just finished my dinner, he called.
And as we connected, I said, I think that I am your sister.
And he said, I don't have a sister.
I say, you don't know, but I could be your sister.
He said, so when were you born?
I said, 1939.
He said, then for sure I'm not your brother.
Because I was born in 1942.
And I said, but how do you know that you were born 1942?
And he said, because my Christian birth certificate says that I was born in 1942.
So then I said, you know what?
My Christian birth certificate says that I was born in 1942.
What does it tell you?
That we were born in the same time, or were Christian at the same time.
He thought about it, and he said, you know what?
If you come to Poland, I'll help you look for your brother.
I was very frustrated.
And he also gave his fax number.
So I took the pictures that I had, and I photocopied it as large as I could at work.
And we have a fax.
And I sent him a fax of the pictures with description.
This is me sitting on my mother lap.
This is you, Adam Paluch.
This is Genia.
This is Aunt Rozia and her son.
And when I send it, immediately I had a respond.
Like, few hours later, he calls.
And he said, you know what?
May I call you Ida?
I said, sure.
Why should we call Mr. and Mrs.?
He said, my son just received your fax.
And he came to me with his picture, and he says, father, this must be your sister or somebody.
Because she looks just like me.
And when, you know, he later showed me the picture, his son at three year old, looks just like I, at three year old, sitting on his-- on our mother's lap.
And then I got more pictures from him.
Because I asked him, send me a picture without the beard.
Send me a picture from the youngest age you can.
And he said, when I was young, I was a blonde.
Were you?
I said, yes.
I was blonde too.
He asked me a few more questions.
And I said, do you remember anything?
He said, no.
But when he sent me a letter, he wrote in it, Ida, I did remember one thing.
When I was little, my parents told me that I was praying, God help father.
God, help mother.
And God help [?
Panya ?] Leona.
And when I heard [?
Pan ?] Leon, I knew this is it.
Because our Father was called Leon.
So I told him, do you realize what you wrote to me?
I called him.
He said, no.
I say, what's [?
Pan ?] Leon?
He said, I thought it's an animal, a lion or something.
I said, you know that our father was called-- called Leon?
And he said, oh God.
He said, you are my sister.
You must be my sister.
And then we decided that one of us has to make a trip.
And he said, it's easier for you to come to Poland than for me to United States.
And I decided that within three months, I'm gonna be in Poland.
It was the hardest three months of my life and his life.
We almost went crazy.
You know, we wanted so much to see each other.
And finally, on April 28, I flew into Warsaw, where I met him.
And the minute I saw him in the airport, when our eyes met, it was like a magnet.
You know, we-- we pulled to each other.
And he said, Ida, Ida, he said, please put me on a seat, because I'm gonna faint.
His nerves were already stretched to the end.
And over there, I-- he prepared.
The television came from Gdansk, and they took a picture of our meeting.
And television came from Israel too.
Because I said they have the same right to do.
There's not only Polish television.
And they took pictures.
And we were in the news.
And friends saw me.
And my aunt Ester saw me in Israel.
And since then on, I stayed in Poland for a while.
And we started to go back to our roots to find out what can we find out.
I was sure that-- that easy I'll have his birth certificate and my birth certificate is there.
It has to be Adam's.
When we went to the Bureau in the City Hall, and I told them the name, they said, there is no such person.
Because I wrote ahead of time that I'm coming to Poland if Adam is there.
In Sosnowiec, In our own city.
When I heard that his birth certificate isn't there, I got hysterical.
I cried so hard.
They had to take me out of the room.
And I said, how is it possible?
And then they went to the archives and brought it.
And said, because your birth certificate was made in 1949.
It's not the original from 1939.
That's what happened.
That's why he doesn't have.
I said, that's impossible.
Look for my sister birth certificate.
She was born probably seven years earlier, whatever.
And they say, all those papers are destroyed.
We did find the birth certificate of another Genia Paluch in 1937.
But that was my uncle in Israel for his daughter that he lost also in Auschwitz.
Because now he has a second family.
He started all over.
I believe-- well, maybe for Genia, yes.
For-- but for sure not for the two of us.
Because it was a statement in the archives from the courts, that he had no time.
Because of the war, we were three months old to make our birth certificate.
So it happened after the war.
But the next time we came, we did not believe that.
We gave them time.
Please search.
And instead of a birth certificate, I got my mother's death certificate.
In one way, I was happy to find it.
But on the other hand, when I saw it black and white, what happened and when, now the chronology of my life was somehow, you know, prearranged now.
Now I knew that this was the time my mother died, that must have been 1942 when I came to my Christian family.
At least I could figure out what was when.
That helped me.
